lines.translation: Come in, please come in.
How come there are so many people? It seems there are no seats left.
Waiter, excuse me, are there any seats available?
Yes, yes, yes. That table is free.
What would you two like to eat?
Wang Peng, why don't you order?
Okay. First, give us two plates of dumplings, vegetarian ones.
Besides dumplings, what else would you like?
Li You, what do you say?
I also want a plate of home-style tofu, without meat. I'm a vegetarian.
Our home-style tofu doesn't have meat.
I also want two bowls of hot and sour soup. Please don't add MSG, and use less salt. Do you have baby bok choy?
Sorry, the baby bok choy is just sold out.
Then we'll do without green vegetables.
What would you like to drink then?
I'd like a glass of iced tea. Li You, what are you drinking?
I'm very thirsty. Please give me a Coke, with extra ice.
Okay, two plates of dumplings, one plate of home-style tofu, two bowls of hot and sour soup, one iced tea, one Coke with extra ice. Anything else?
Nothing else, this is enough. Waiter, we are both hungry, please serve the food a little faster.
No problem, the dishes will be ready soon.

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: In Beijing, 这么 (zhème) is commonly pronounced as zème.

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.explanation_en: Seat is 位子 (wèizi); chair is 椅子 (yǐzi).

language_notes.label: c
language_notes.explanation_en: Nouns for objects that contain things can function as measure words, as in 一碗饭 (yì wǎn fàn) (a bowl of rice), 一杯水 (yì bēi shuǐ) (a glass of water), and 一盘饺子 (yì pán jiǎozi) (a plate of dumplings). When these words are used as nouns rather than as measure words, some have to take a suffix such as 子 (zi), as in 杯子 (bēizi) (cup), 盘子 (pánzi) (plate), and 瓶子 (píngzi) (bottle). Note that these words are defined in the vocabulary list according to the part of speech in which they appear in the text.

language_notes.label: d
language_notes.explanation_en: When used as an adjective, 够 (gòu) can only be a predicate; it cannot come before the noun being modified. Thus you can say 我的钱不够 (Wǒ de qián bú gòu) (I don't have enough money), but never 我没有够钱. In an affirmative statement, 够 (gòu) usually cannot be modified by 很 (hěn). Additionally, 够 (gòu) can be used after a verb as a complement, e.g.: 玩(儿)够了 (wán[r] gòu le) (to have played enough) and 买够了 (mǎi gòu le) (to have bought enough).

language_notes.label: e
language_notes.explanation_en: 请上菜快一点儿 (qǐng shàng cài kuài yì diǎnr) is a topic-comment sentence. [See also Grammar 1, Lesson 10, Volume 1.] 上菜 (shàng cài) is known information and the topic of the sentence. 快一点儿 (kuài yì diǎnr) is new information, hence the word order.

grammar_sections.title: 一…也/都…不/没… (yī...yě/dōu...bù/méi...)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: These structures are used for emphatic negation; they express the meaning "not at all" or "not even one."

patterns.pattern: Subject + 一 + measure word + object + 也/都 + 不/没(有) (+ verb)
patterns.pattern_pinyin: Subject + yī + measure word + object + yě/dōu + bù/méi(yǒu) (+ verb)
patterns.meaning_en: Used with countable nouns to express 'not even one'.

patterns.pattern: Topic (object) + subject + 一 + measure word + 也/都 + 不/没 + verb
patterns.pattern_pinyin: Topic (object) + subject + yī + measure word + yě/dōu + bù/méi + verb
patterns.meaning_en: Used with a fronted object (topic) to express 'not even one'.

patterns.pattern: Subject + 一点儿 + object + 也/都 + 不/没 + verb
patterns.pattern_pinyin: Subject + yì diǎnr + object + yě/dōu + bù/méi + verb
patterns.meaning_en: Used with uncountable nouns to express 'not a bit' or 'not at all'.

patterns.pattern: Topic + 一点儿 + 也/都 + 不 + Adjective
patterns.pattern_pinyin: Topic + yì diǎnr + yě/dōu + bù + Adjective
patterns.meaning_en: Used before an adjective to express 'not... at all'.

examples.hanzi: 小李一个朋友也没有。
examples.pinyin: Xiǎo Lǐ yí ge péngyou yě méiyǒu.
examples.translation: Little Li doesn't have a single friend.

examples.hanzi: 爸爸今天一杯茶都没喝。
examples.pinyin: Bàba jīntiān yì bēi chá dōu méi hē.
examples.translation: My father didn't have a single cup of tea today.

examples.hanzi: 这些衬衫我一件也不喜欢。
examples.pinyin: Zhè xiē chènshān wǒ yí jiàn yě bù xǐhuan.
examples.translation: I don't like any of these shirts.

examples.hanzi: 哥哥的鞋,弟弟一双都不能穿。
examples.pinyin: Gēge de xié, dìdi yì shuāng dōu bù néng chuān.
examples.translation: The younger brother cannot wear a single pair of his older brother's shoes.

examples.hanzi: 他去了商店,可是一点儿东西也没买。
examples.pinyin: Tā qù le shāngdiàn, kěshì yì diǎnr dōngxi yě méi mǎi.
examples.translation: He went to the store, but didn't buy anything at all.

examples.hanzi: 妈妈做菜一点儿味精都不放。
examples.pinyin: Māma zuò cài yì diǎnr wèijīng dōu bú fàng.
examples.translation: Mom doesn't use any MSG in her cooking.

examples.hanzi: 这儿的冬天一点儿也不冷。
examples.pinyin: Zhèr de dōngtiān yì diǎnr yě bù lěng.
examples.translation: Winter here isn't cold at all.

examples.hanzi: 那个学校一点儿也不漂亮。
examples.pinyin: Nà ge xuéxiào yì diǎnr yě bú piàoliang.
examples.translation: That school is not pretty at all.

examples.hanzi: 这杯冰茶一点都不好喝。
examples.pinyin: Zhè bēi bīngchá yì diǎnr dōu bù hǎohē.
examples.translation: This glass of iced tea doesn't taste good at all.

usage_notes: If the noun after 一 (yī) is countable, a proper measure word should be used between 一 (yī) and the noun, as in (A), (B), (C), and (D).
If the noun is uncountable, the phrase 一点儿 (yì diǎnr) is usually used instead, as in (E) and (F).
The construction 一点儿 (yìdiǎnr) + 也/都 (yě/dōu) + 不/没 (bù/méi) can also be used before an adjective to express emphatic negation, as in (G), (H), and (I).

common_errors.explanation_en: Incorrect word order. The phrase '一 + measure word + noun' should be placed before '也/都 + 没'.

common_errors.wrong: 这些衬衫我不喜欢一件。
common_errors.correct: 这些衬衫我一件也不喜欢。
common_errors.explanation_en: Incorrect word order. The phrase '一 + measure word' should be placed before '也/都 + 不'.
Incorrect word order. The phrase '一点儿 + noun' should be placed before '也/都 + 没'.

grammar_sections.title: 多/少 (duō/shǎo) + verb
grammar_sections.explanation_en: The way the two adjectives 多 (duō) and 少 (shǎo) are used is non-typical of adjectives in general. To express doing something “more” or “less,” place 多 (duō) or 少 (shǎo) before the verb. The “多/少 (duō/shǎo) + verb” construction is sometimes used to denote a deviation from the correct amount or number.

patterns.meaning_en: To do something more or less; to do something in excess or insufficiently.

examples.hanzi: 爸爸告诉妈妈做菜的时候少放盐，多放点儿糖。
examples.pinyin: Bàba gàosu māma zuò cài de shíhou shǎo fàng yán, duō fàng diǎnr táng.
examples.translation: Dad asked Mom to add less salt and more sugar when cooking.

examples.hanzi: 上中文课得多说中文，少说英文。
examples.pinyin: Shàng Zhōngwén kè děi duō shuō Zhōngwén, shǎo shuō Yīngwén.
examples.translation: In Chinese class, one should speak more Chinese and less English.

examples.hanzi: 你多找了我一块钱。
examples.pinyin: Nǐ duō zhǎo le wǒ yí kuài qián.
examples.translation: You gave me one dollar too many.

examples.hanzi: 老师说要写五十个字，我写了四十五个，少写了五个。
examples.pinyin: Lǎoshī shuō yào xiě wǔshí ge zì, wǒ xiě le sìshíwǔ ge, shǎo xiě le wǔ ge.
examples.translation: The teacher told us to write fifty characters. I wrote forty-five. I was five short.

grammar_sections.title: Comparing 刚 (gāng) (just) and 刚才 (gāngcái) (just now)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: As an adverb, 刚 (gāng) (just) denotes that an action or change in situation took place in the very recent past. 刚才 (gāngcái) (just now) is a time word that refers to the period just moments before. Although 刚 (gāng) and 刚才 (gāngcái) are similar in meaning, they are classified as different parts of speech and are therefore used differently. First, unlike 刚才 (gāngcái), 刚 (gāng) cannot be followed by 不 (bù) or 没 (méi). Second, sentences that include 刚才 (gāngcái) often end with 了 (le). By contrast, sentences that include 刚 (gāng) cannot end with 了 (le).

examples.hanzi: 我哥哥刚从中国来，在这儿一个朋友都没有。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ gēge gāng cóng Zhōngguó lái, zài zhèr yí ge péngyou dōu méiyǒu.
examples.translation: My older brother just came from China. He doesn't have a single friend here.

examples.hanzi: 我刚洗完澡，舒服极了。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ gāng xǐ wán zǎo, shūfu jí le.
examples.translation: I just showered, and feel great.

examples.hanzi:
Q: 你知道王朋在哪儿吗？
A: 他刚才在这儿，我不知道他去哪儿了。
examples.pinyin:
Q: Nǐ zhīdào Wáng Péng zài nǎr ma?
A: Tā gāngcái zài zhèr, wǒ bù zhīdao tā qù nǎr le.
examples.translation:
Q: Do you know where Wang Peng is?
A: He was here a moment ago. I don't know where he went.

examples.hanzi: 弟弟刚才吃了十五个饺子，喝了两碗酸辣汤。
examples.pinyin: Dìdi gāngcái chī le shíwǔ ge jiǎozi, hē le liǎng wǎn suānlàtāng.
examples.translation: My younger brother ate fifteen dumplings and two bowls of hot-and-sour soup a moment ago.

examples.hanzi:
Q: 你刚才为什么没说？
A: 我刚才不想说。
examples.pinyin:
Q: Nǐ gāngcái wèishénme méi shuō?
A: Wǒ gāngcái bù xiǎng shuō.
examples.translation:
Q: Why didn't you say anything a moment ago?
A: I didn't want to say anything a moment ago.

examples.hanzi:
Q: 你刚才去哪儿了？老师要你去办公室找他。
A: 我刚才去图书馆了。
examples.pinyin:
Q: Nǐ gāngcái qù nǎr le? Lǎoshī yào nǐ qù bàngōngshì zhǎo tā.
A: Wǒ gāngcái qù túshūguǎn le.
examples.translation:
Q: Where were you a moment ago? The teacher wanted you to go to his office.
A: I went to the library just now.

examples.hanzi:
Q: 明天的考试你开始准备了吗？
A: 刚开始准备。
examples.pinyin:
Q: Míngtiān de kǎoshì nǐ kāishǐ zhǔnbèi le ma?
A: Gāng kāishǐ zhǔnbèi.
examples.translation:
Q: Have you started preparing for tomorrow's test?
A: I just started.

usage_notes: 刚 (gāng) is an adverb denoting an action in the very recent past.
刚才 (gāngcái) is a time word referring to the period just moments before.
刚 (gāng) cannot be followed by a negative word like 不 (bù) or 没 (méi).
Sentences with 刚才 (gāngcái) often end with 了 (le), but sentences with 刚 (gāng) cannot.

common_errors.explanation_en: 刚 (gāng) cannot be followed by the negative word 没 (méi).
刚 (gāng) cannot be followed by the negative word 不 (bù).
Sentences that include 刚 (gāng) cannot end with 了 (le).

grammar_sections.title: Resultative complements (I)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Following a verb, an adjective or another verb can be used to denote the result of the action, hence the term resultative complement. Generally, the negative form of a resultative complement is formed by placing 没 (méi) (no, not) or 没有 (méiyǒu) (have not) before the verb.

examples.hanzi: 小白菜卖完了。
examples.pinyin: Xiǎo báicài mài wán le.
examples.translation: The baby bok choy is sold out.

examples.hanzi: 你找错钱了。
examples.pinyin: Nǐ zhǎo cuò qián le.
examples.translation: You gave me the wrong change.

examples.hanzi: 那个人是谁你看清楚了吗？
examples.pinyin: Nà ge rén shì shéi nǐ kàn qīngchu le ma?
examples.translation: Did you see clearly who that person was?

examples.hanzi: 太好了，这个字你写对了。
examples.pinyin: Tài hǎo le, zhè ge zì nǐ xiě duì le.
examples.translation: Great! You wrote this character correctly.

examples.hanzi: 小白菜没卖完。
examples.pinyin: Xiǎo báicài méi mài wán.
examples.translation: The baby bok choy isn't sold out.

examples.hanzi: 那个人我没看清楚。
examples.pinyin: Nà ge rén wǒ méi kàn qīngchu.
examples.translation: I didn't see clearly who that person was.

examples.hanzi: 糟糕，这个字你没有写对。
examples.pinyin: Zāogāo, zhè ge zì nǐ méiyǒu xiě duì.
examples.translation: Yikes! You didn't write this character correctly.

usage_notes: The use of an adjective as a resultative complement is not random. It is advisable to take the combination of the verb and the complement as a whole unit.

grammar_sections.title: 好 (hǎo) as a resultative complement
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 好 (hǎo) can serve as a complement following a verb to signify the completion of an action and readiness to start another.

patterns.meaning_en: Indicates that an action is completed and one is ready for the next action.

examples.hanzi: 饭做好了, 快来吃吧。
examples.pinyin: Fàn zuò hǎo le, kuài lái chī ba.
examples.translation: The food is ready. Come and eat.

examples.hanzi: 功课做好了, 我要睡觉了。
examples.pinyin: Gōngkè zuò hǎo le, wǒ yào shuì jiào le.
examples.translation: My homework is done, and I'm going to bed.

examples.hanzi: 衣服我已经帮你买好了, 明天晚会你就可以穿了。
examples.pinyin: Yīfu wǒ yǐjīng bāng nǐ mǎi hǎo le, míngtiān wǎnhuì nǐ jiù kěyǐ chuān le.
examples.translation: I've already bought clothes for you. You can wear them to the party tomorrow night.

drill_sentences.cn: 那儿的衬衫他/她都不喜欢，一件都没买。
drill_sentences.py: Nàr de chènshān tā dōu bù xǐhuan, yí jiàn dōu méi mǎi.
drill_sentences.translation: He/She doesn't like the shirts there, and didn't buy a single one.

language_practice_sections.title: B. One of those days
language_practice_sections.focus_pattern: Verb + Resultative Complement (e.g., 错, 懂, 完, 见) + 了 (le)

language_practice_sections.title: C. Getting it done
language_practice_sections.focus_pattern: Using 好 (hǎo) as a resultative complement to ask and answer about the completion of a task.

drill_sentences.py: Nǐ Hànzì liànxí hǎo le ma?
drill_sentences.translation: Have you finished practicing the Chinese characters?
I have finished practicing the Chinese characters.

global_config.visual_style: Cinematic, realistic, warm lighting, shallow depth of field, vibrant colors.
global_config.setting_summary_cn: 在一个热闹、传统的中餐馆里，两个年轻朋友李友和王朋进来找位子点餐。
global_config.setting_summary_en: Two young friends, Li You and Wang Peng, enter a bustling, traditional Chinese restaurant to find a table and order food.

character_definitions.name: 李友
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A young Chinese woman in her early 20s, with shoulder-length black hair, wearing a casual sweater and jeans, looking curious and slightly overwhelmed.

character_definitions.name: 王朋
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A young Chinese man in his early 20s, with short black hair, wearing a hoodie and glasses, looking friendly and proactive.

character_definitions.name: 服务员
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A middle-aged Chinese man in his 40s, wearing a simple restaurant uniform (white shirt, black apron), looking busy but welcoming.

scenes.scene_goal: 介绍场景和人物，服务员欢迎顾客。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 镜头从餐馆门口开始，李友和王朋推门而入。一位忙碌的服务员看到他们，立刻面带微笑，热情地招手让他们进来。背景是嘈杂但温馨的用餐环境。
scenes.setting_en: The entrance of a bustling, traditional Chinese restaurant. Red lanterns, lively chatter. It's dinner time and the place is packed.
scenes.camera_movement: Static shot, then a slight pan to follow the characters.
scenes.video_prompt_en: A young Chinese couple, Li You and Wang Peng, enter a bustling, warmly lit Chinese restaurant. A busy waiter in an apron spots them, smiles, and gestures for them to come in. Cinematic, realistic style.

scenes.setting_en: Inside the crowded main dining hall of the Chinese restaurant.
scenes.camera_movement: Pan from Li You's face to a sweeping shot of the crowded restaurant (her POV), then back to her.
scenes.shot_type: Close-up on Li You, then a POV shot

global_config.teaching_style: A clear, step-by-step breakdown of dialogue, focusing on vocabulary and practical usage in a restaurant setting. The tone is encouraging and supportive.
global_config.visual_style: Clean, modern, with animated text and icons to support learning.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand and use a common welcome phrase in a restaurant.

narration.subtitle_en: The dialogue begins with the waiter welcoming the customers. They say [zh:请进,请进]. [zh:请] means 'please,' and [zh:进] means 'to enter.' So, [zh:请进] literally means 'please enter.' Repeating it makes the welcome sound more enthusiastic and friendly. This is a very common and polite way to greet customers entering a shop or restaurant.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 请进,请进。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: qǐng jìn, qǐng jìn.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Come in, please come in.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A polite word used to make requests or invite someone to do something.
A verb indicating movement into a space.

segments.visual_notes: Animation of a restaurant door opening with the characters walking in.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express surprise about a situation and observe a lack of something.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You is surprised by how busy the restaurant is. She says, [zh:人怎么这么多]. This means 'How come there are so many people?'. [zh:怎么] here expresses surprise. Then she says, [zh:好像一个位子都没有了]. [zh:好像] means 'it seems,' and [zh:位子] means 'seat.' The full phrase means 'It seems there isn't a single seat left.' This is an emphatic way to say there are no seats.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 人怎么这么多？好像一个位子都没有了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: rén zěnme zhème duō? hǎoxiàng yí ge wèizi dōu méiyǒu le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: How come there are so many people? It seems there are no seats left.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used here to express surprise or ask for a reason.
Used to express an impression or observation.
Refers to a place to sit, especially in a public venue like a restaurant or theater.

segments.visual_notes: Show a bustling restaurant scene. Highlight the phrase '一个...都没' to draw attention to the structure.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to get a waiter's attention and ask if seats are available.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng addresses the waiter to ask for a table. He starts with [zh:服务员], which means 'waiter' or 'attendant.' Then he politely asks, [zh:请问,还有没有位子]. [zh:请问] is 'excuse me, may I ask...'. The question [zh:还有没有位子] uses the 'verb-not-verb' structure to ask 'are there still seats or not?'. It's a very common way to ask a yes-no question.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 服务员,请问,还有没有位子？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: fúwùyuán, qǐngwèn, hái yǒu méiyǒu wèizi?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Waiter, excuse me, are there any seats available?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A general term for service staff in places like restaurants, hotels, or airports.
Refers to a place to sit.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a character raising their hand to call the waiter. Highlight the '有没有' structure.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a positive response and how to identify an object using measure words.

narration.subtitle_en: The waiter responds positively. The triple [zh:有,有,有] is an emphatic 'Yes, yes, yes!'. Then, the waiter points out a free table: [zh:那张桌子没有人]. [zh:那] means 'that.' [zh:张] is a measure word used for flat objects like tables, paper, or beds. [zh:桌子] is 'table.' The phrase [zh:没有人] literally means 'has no people,' which in this context means the table is free or unoccupied.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 有,有,有。那张桌子没有人。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: yǒu, yǒu, yǒu. nà zhāng zhuōzi méiyǒu rén.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Yes, yes, yes. That table is free.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used for tables, paper, beds, etc.
A piece of furniture with a flat top and one or more legs.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the waiter pointing to an empty table. A label with '那张桌子' appears above the table.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the standard phrase a waiter uses to ask what customers want to eat.

narration.subtitle_en: Now that they are seated, the waiter asks what they'd like to eat. The phrase is [zh:两位想吃点儿什么]. Let's break it down. [zh:两] means 'two.' [zh:位] is a polite measure word for people. [zh:想] means 'would like to.' [zh:吃点儿什么] means 'eat a little something?'. This is a very common and polite way for service staff to ask for your order.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 两位想吃点儿什么？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: liǎng wèi xiǎng chī diǎnr shénme?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: What would you two like to eat?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used instead of '二' (èr) when counting people or things with a measure word.
A more formal and polite alternative to '个' (ge) when referring to people.

segments.visual_notes: Show the waiter holding a notepad, ready to take the order. Highlight '两位' to emphasize the polite form.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to suggest someone else do something.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You suggests that Wang Peng should order the food. She says, [zh:你点菜吧]. The key phrase here is [zh:点菜], which is a verb-object compound meaning 'to order dishes.' The particle [zh:吧] at the end of the sentence softens the tone and turns it into a suggestion, like 'Why don't you order?' or 'Go ahead and order.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 王朋,你点菜吧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wáng Péng, nǐ diǎn cài ba.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Wang Peng, why don't you order?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'to point at dishes,' this is the standard verb for ordering food from a menu.

segments.visual_notes: Animate Li You gesturing towards Wang Peng with the menu.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to place an order for a specific dish and specify a preference.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng starts ordering. He says, [zh:先给我们两盘饺子]. [zh:先] means 'first.' [zh:两盘饺子] means 'two plates of dumplings.' [zh:盘] is the measure word for 'plate.' Then he adds a specification: [zh:要素的]. [zh:要] means 'to want,' and [zh:素的] means 'vegetarian ones.' This is a concise way to specify that he wants the vegetarian version of the dish.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好。先给我们两盘饺子,要素的。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hǎo. xiān gěi wǒmen liǎng pán jiǎozi, yào sù de.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Okay. First, give us two plates of dumplings, vegetarian ones.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used for food served on a plate.
A popular Chinese dish consisting of minced meat and/or vegetables wrapped in a thin piece of dough.
Used to describe food that does not contain meat.

segments.visual_notes: Show an image of two plates of dumplings. A 'vegetarian' icon can pop up next to them.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a common structure for asking 'besides this, what else?'.

narration.subtitle_en: The waiter asks what else they would like. The sentence uses the structure [zh:除了...以外]. This means 'besides' or 'in addition to.' So, [zh:除了饺子以外] means 'besides the dumplings.' The question [zh:还要什么] means 'what else do you want?'. This is a very useful pattern for adding more items to an order or list.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 除了饺子以外,还要什么？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: chúle jiǎozi yǐwài, hái yào shénme?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Besides dumplings, what else would you like?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A structure used to talk about something in addition to what has already been mentioned.
Indicates continuation or addition.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the '除了...以外' structure, showing how it frames '饺子'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a simple phrase to ask for someone's opinion.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng turns to Li You to ask for her input. He says, [zh:李友, 你说呢]. This is a very simple and common way to ask for someone's opinion. [zh:你说] means 'you say,' and the particle [zh:呢] turns it into a question, similar to 'What do you say?' or 'And you?'. It's a way of passing the turn to someone else.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 李友,你说呢？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lǐ Yǒu, nǐ shuō ne?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Li You, what do you say?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common verb for speaking.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a question mark appearing over Li You's head.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to order another dish, make a negative request, and state a dietary preference.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You adds her order. She wants [zh:一盘家常豆腐], one plate of home-style tofu. Then she makes a special request: [zh:不要放肉], which means 'don't put meat in it.' [zh:放] is the verb 'to put.' Finally, she explains why: [zh:我吃素]. This phrase means 'I eat vegetarian' or 'I am a vegetarian.' It's a very important phrase for anyone with dietary restrictions.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 还要一盘家常豆腐,不要放肉,我吃素。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hái yào yì pán jiācháng dòufu, bú yào fàng ròu, wǒ chīsù.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I also want a plate of home-style tofu, without meat. I'm a vegetarian.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common tofu dish. '家常' means 'home-style'.
A verb used here to mean 'to add an ingredient'.
A general term for meat.
Literally 'to eat vegetarian food'.

segments.visual_notes: Show an image of home-style tofu. Animate a 'no meat' icon appearing next to it.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a simple sentence confirming the contents of a dish.

narration.subtitle_en: The waiter confirms that the dish meets Li You's requirement. They say, [zh:我们的家常豆腐没有肉]. This is a straightforward sentence. [zh:我们] means 'our.' [zh:的] is a possessive particle. So, [zh:我们的家常豆腐] means 'our home-style tofu.' The phrase [zh:没有肉] simply means 'doesn't have meat.' This reassures Li You that the dish is vegetarian.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我们的家常豆腐没有肉。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒmen de jiācháng dòufu méiyǒu ròu.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Our home-style tofu doesn't have meat.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The negative form of '有' (yǒu), meaning 'to have'.

segments.visual_notes: Show the waiter giving a thumbs-up or a reassuring nod.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to order soup and make multiple specific requests about ingredients.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You continues her order with more details. First, [zh:还要两碗酸辣汤] means 'also want two bowls of hot and sour soup.' [zh:碗] is the measure word for 'bowl.' Then come two requests: [zh:请别放味精], 'please don't add MSG,' and [zh:少放点儿盐], 'add a little less salt.' Notice the use of [zh:少] to mean 'less.' Finally, she asks, [zh:有小白菜吗], 'Do you have baby bok choy?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 还要两碗酸辣汤,请别放味精,少放点儿盐。有小白菜吗？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hái yào liǎng wǎn suānlàtāng, qǐng bié fàng wèijīng, shǎo fàng diǎnr yán. yǒu xiǎo báicài ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Also want two bowls of hot and sour soup. Please don't add MSG, and use less salt. Do you have baby bok choy?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used for food served in a bowl, like soup or rice.
A popular Chinese soup. '酸' means sour, '辣' means spicy, and '汤' means soup.
A common flavor enhancer.
Used before a verb to mean 'do something less'.
A common seasoning.
A type of small Chinese cabbage.

segments.visual_notes: Show icons for 'no MSG' and 'less salt' next to a picture of the soup.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to say something is sold out using a resultative complement.

narration.subtitle_en: The waiter apologizes because they don't have the vegetable. They say, [zh:对不起], which means 'sorry.' Then, [zh:小白菜刚卖完]. [zh:刚] means 'just now.' The key part is [zh:卖完]. This is a verb with a resultative complement. [zh:卖] means 'to sell,' and [zh:完] means 'finished' or 'done.' Together, [zh:卖完] means 'to be sold out.' This is a very common and useful structure.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 对不起,小白菜刚卖完。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: duìbuqǐ, xiǎo báicài gāng mài wán.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Sorry, the baby bok choy is just sold out.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb indicating that an action took place in the very recent past.
The verb for selling goods.
Used as a complement after a verb to indicate the completion of an action.

segments.visual_notes: Show a picture of bok choy with a 'Sold Out' sign stamped over it.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to cancel part of an order.

narration.subtitle_en: Since the bok choy is unavailable, Li You decides to skip the vegetables. She says, [zh:那就不要青菜了]. [zh:那就] means 'in that case' or 'then.' [zh:不要] means 'don't want.' [zh:青菜] is a general term for green leafy vegetables. The particle [zh:了] at the end indicates a change of situation or decision. So, the sentence means 'Then we'll do without green vegetables.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 那就不要青菜了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nà jiù bú yào qīngcài le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Then we'll do without green vegetables.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A general term for vegetables like bok choy, spinach, etc.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the bok choy being crossed off the order list.

segments.teaching_goal: To recognize the standard question for asking about drinks.

narration.subtitle_en: With the food order settled, the waiter moves on to drinks. They ask, [zh:那喝点儿什么呢]. [zh:那] means 'then' or 'in that case.' The core question is [zh:喝点儿什么], 'drink a little something?'. [zh:喝] is the verb 'to drink.' The particle [zh:呢] at the end makes the question softer and more conversational. This is the standard way to ask what someone wants to drink.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 那喝点儿什么呢？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nà hē diǎnr shénme ne?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: What would you like to drink then?

highlight_words.explanation_en: The verb for consuming liquids.

segments.visual_notes: Show icons of various drinks like tea, soda, and water.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to order a drink and ask someone else what they want.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng orders his drink first. He says, [zh:我要一杯冰茶]. [zh:我要] means 'I want.' [zh:一杯] means 'one glass of.' [zh:杯] is the measure word for 'cup' or 'glass.' [zh:冰茶] is 'iced tea.' After ordering for himself, he asks Li You, [zh:你喝什么], which simply means 'What are you drinking?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我要一杯冰茶。李友,你喝什么？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ yào yī bēi bīngchá. Lǐ Yǒu, nǐ hē shénme?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I'd like a glass of iced tea. Li You, what are you drinking?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used for drinks served in a cup or glass.
'冰' means ice, and '茶' means tea.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a glass of iced tea appearing next to Wang Peng.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express thirst and make a specific request for 'more' of something.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You explains she is thirsty: [zh:我很渴]. [zh:渴] is the adjective for 'thirsty.' Then she orders: [zh:请给我一杯可乐], 'Please give me a glass of Coke.' She adds a special request: [zh:多放点儿冰]. [zh:多] means 'more.' Used before the verb [zh:放] (to put), it means 'put more' or 'add extra.' [zh:冰] means 'ice.' So, she wants extra ice in her drink.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我很渴,请给我一杯可乐,多放点儿冰。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ hěn kě, qǐng gěi wǒ yī bēi kělè, duō fàng diǎnr bīng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I'm very thirsty. Please give me a Coke, with extra ice.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The feeling of needing to drink.
A phonetic borrowing for 'cola'.
Used before a verb to mean 'do something more'.
Frozen water.

segments.visual_notes: Show a glass of coke being filled with extra ice cubes.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how a waiter confirms a full order and asks a final confirmation question.

narration.subtitle_en: The waiter repeats the entire order to confirm it's correct. This is a great review of all the food items and measure words: [zh:两盘饺子], [zh:一盘家常豆腐], [zh:两碗酸辣汤], [zh:一杯冰茶], and [zh:一杯可乐]. They also confirm the special request, [zh:多放冰]. Finally, they ask one last time, [zh:还要别的吗], which means 'Anything else?'. [zh:别的] means 'other things'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好,两盘饺子,一盘家常豆腐,两碗酸辣汤,一杯冰茶,一杯可乐,多放冰。还要别的吗？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hǎo, liǎng pán jiǎozi, yī pán jiācháng dòufu, liǎng wǎn suānlàtāng, yī bēi bīngchá, yī bēi kělè, duō fàng bīng. hái yào biéde ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Okay, two plates of dumplings, one plate of home-style tofu, two bowls of hot and sour soup, one iced tea, one Coke with extra ice. Anything else?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to other, different things.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a checklist appearing on screen, with each item being ticked off as the waiter says it.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to confirm an order is complete and ask for faster service.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You confirms the order is complete and makes a request. She says, [zh:不要别的了], 'Nothing else,' and [zh:这些够了], 'This is enough.' [zh:够] means 'enough.' Then she adds that they are hungry: [zh:我们都饿了]. [zh:饿] means 'hungry.' Because they are hungry, she asks, [zh:请上菜快一点儿]. [zh:上菜] means 'to serve the food,' and [zh:快一点儿] means 'a little faster.' This is a polite way to ask for quick service.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 不要别的了,这些够了。服务员,我们都饿了,请上菜快一点儿。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: bú yào bié de le, zhè xiē gòu le. fú wù yuán, wǒ men dōu è le, qǐng shàng cài kuài yì diǎnr.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Nothing else, this is enough. Waiter, we are both hungry, please serve the food a little faster.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates that the quantity is sufficient.
The feeling of needing to eat.
Literally 'to bring up the dishes'.
An adjective for speed.

segments.visual_notes: Show a character's stomach rumbling. Then show a clock with its hands speeding up.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a common phrase for 'no problem' and how to say something will be ready soon.

narration.subtitle_en: The waiter reassures them. They start with [zh:没问题], a very common and useful phrase that means 'no problem.' Then they say, [zh:菜很快就能做好]. [zh:菜] is 'dishes.' [zh:很快] means 'very soon.' The final part, [zh:做好], means 'to be finished' or 'to be ready.' The verb [zh:做] means 'to make,' and the complement [zh:好] indicates the action is completed well and ready for the next step, which is eating!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 没问题,菜很快就能做好。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: méi wèn tí, cài hěn kuài jiù néng zuò hǎo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: No problem, the dishes will be ready soon.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common expression used to agree to a request or to reassure someone.
'好' is used as a resultative complement to indicate that the action '做' (to do/make) is completed successfully.

segments.visual_notes: The waiter gives a confident smile and a thumbs-up. An animation shows delicious food being prepared quickly in the kitchen.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students how to express 'not at all' or 'not even one' for emphatic negation.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look at our first grammar point: emphatic negation. In the dialogue, Li You says [zh:好像一个位子都没有了], meaning 'It seems there isn't a single seat left.' This structure, '一 + measure word + noun + 都/也 + 没/不', is used to strongly negate something. It means 'not even one'. For countable nouns, you must use a measure word, like [zh:个] for [zh:位子]. For uncountable nouns, you can use [zh:一点儿]. For example, [zh:妈妈做菜一点儿味精都不放] means 'Mom doesn't use any MSG at all in her cooking.' This is a very common and useful pattern to emphasize a total lack of something.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 一个 + [Measure Word] + [Noun] + 都/也 + 没有
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: yí ge + [Measure Word] + [Noun] + dōu/yě + méiyǒu
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Not even one [Noun]
on_screen_text.notes: Used to express 'not at all' or 'not even one'.

grammar_points.pattern: 好像一个位子都没有了。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: hǎoxiàng yí ge wèizi dōu méiyǒu le
grammar_points.explanation_en: It seems there isn't a single seat left. (With countable noun)

grammar_points.pattern: 爸爸今天一杯茶都没喝。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Bàba jīntiān yì bēi chá dōu méi hē.
grammar_points.explanation_en: My father didn't have a single cup of tea today. (With countable noun)

grammar_points.pattern: 妈妈做菜一点儿味精都不放。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Māma zuò cài yì diǎnr wèijīng dōu bú fàng.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Mom doesn't use any MSG in her cooking. (With uncountable noun)

grammar_points.pattern: 这儿的冬天一点儿也不冷。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Zhèr de dōngtiān yì diǎnr yě bù lěng.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Winter here isn't cold at all. (With adjective)

segments.visual_notes: Animate the structure on screen, highlighting each component. Use icons for 'seat', 'tea', and 'MSG' next to the example sentences.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students how to use '多' and '少' before verbs to mean 'do more' or 'do less' of an action.

narration.subtitle_en: Next, let's learn how to say 'do more' or 'do less' of something. In Chinese, you place the words [zh:多] for 'more' or [zh:少] for 'less' directly before the verb. In the dialogue, Li You asks the waiter to [zh:少放点儿盐], 'add less salt,' and [zh:多放点儿冰], 'add more ice.' This is different from English where 'more' or 'less' often comes after the verb. Remember the structure: [zh:多] or [zh:少] plus the verb. For example, in Chinese class, your teacher might say [zh:上中文课得多说中文，少说英文], meaning 'In Chinese class, you should speak more Chinese and less English.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: [多/少] + Verb
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: [duō/shǎo] + Verb
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Do more/less of an action

grammar_points.pattern: 请别放味精, 少放点儿盐。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: qǐng bié fàng wèijīng, shǎo fàng diǎnr yán
grammar_points.explanation_en: Please don't add MSG, and use less salt.

grammar_points.pattern: 请给我一杯可乐, 多放点儿冰。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: qǐng gěi wǒ yī bēi kělè, duō fàng diǎnr bīng
grammar_points.explanation_en: Please give me a Coke, with extra ice.

grammar_points.pattern: 上中文课得多说中文, 少说英文。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Shàng Zhōngwén kè děi duō shuō Zhōngwén, shǎo shuō Yīngwén.
grammar_points.explanation_en: In Chinese class, one should speak more Chinese and less English.

segments.visual_notes: Show a plus sign (+) icon for '多' and a minus sign (-) icon for '少' next to the characters. Animate the characters moving to a position before the verb.

segments.teaching_goal: To help students differentiate between the adverb '刚' (just) and the time word '刚才' (just now).

narration.subtitle_en: Now let's clarify two similar-sounding words: [zh:刚] and [zh:刚才]. Both relate to the recent past, but they are used differently. [zh:刚] is an adverb meaning 'just,' and it describes an action that happened a very short time ago. In the dialogue, the waiter says [zh:小白菜刚卖完], 'The baby bok choy just sold out.' In contrast, [zh:刚才] is a time word meaning 'just now' or 'a moment ago.' A key difference is that you cannot use negative words like [zh:不] or [zh:没] after [zh:刚]. Also, sentences with [zh:刚] cannot end with [zh:了]. For example, you can say [zh:他刚才在这儿], 'He was here a moment ago,' but not [zh:他刚在这儿了].

on_screen_text.focus_text: Q: 小白菜还有吗？ A: 小白菜刚卖完。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Q: Xiǎo báicài hái yǒu ma? A: Xiǎo báicài gāng mài wán.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Q: Is there any baby bok choy left? A: The baby bok choy just sold out.
on_screen_text.notes: 刚 (gāng) is an adverb (just); 刚才 (gāngcái) is a time word (just now).

grammar_points.pattern: 我哥哥刚从中国来。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒ gēge gāng cóng Zhōngguó lái.
grammar_points.explanation_en: My older brother just came from China. (Using 刚)

grammar_points.pattern: 他刚才在这儿。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā gāngcái zài zhèr.
grammar_points.explanation_en: He was here a moment ago. (Using 刚才)

grammar_points.pattern: Correct: 你刚才为什么没说？
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Nǐ gāngcái wèishénme méi shuō?
grammar_points.explanation_en: Incorrect to say: 你刚为什么没说？

grammar_points.pattern: Correct: 刚开始准备。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Gāng kāishǐ zhǔnbèi.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Incorrect to say: 刚开始准备了。

segments.visual_notes: Use a split screen to compare '刚' and '刚才' with columns for 'Part of Speech', 'Usage Rules', and 'Examples'.

segments.teaching_goal: To introduce the concept of resultative complements, which indicate the result of a verb's action.

narration.subtitle_en: A very important concept in Chinese grammar is the resultative complement. This is when an adjective or another verb follows a verb to show the result of the action. Let's look at the line [zh:小白菜刚卖完]. Here, the verb is [zh:卖], 'to sell,' and the complement is [zh:完], 'to finish.' Together, [zh:卖完] means 'to sell out'—the action of selling is finished. Other common complements include [zh:错] for 'wrong,' as in [zh:找错钱了], 'gave the wrong change,' and [zh:对] for 'correct,' as in [zh:写对了], 'wrote correctly.' The negative form is simple: just put [zh:没] or [zh:没有] before the verb, like [zh:小白菜没卖完], 'The baby bok choy is not sold out.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: Verb + Resultative Complement
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Indicates the result of an action
on_screen_text.notes: Negative form: 没(有) + Verb + Complement

grammar_points.pattern: 小白菜卖完了。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Xiǎo báicài mài wán le.
grammar_points.explanation_en: The baby bok choy is sold out. (Affirmative)

grammar_points.pattern: 小白菜没卖完。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Xiǎo báicài méi mài wán.
grammar_points.explanation_en: The baby bok choy isn't sold out. (Negative)

grammar_points.pattern: 这个字你写对了。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: zhè ge zì nǐ xiě duì le.
grammar_points.explanation_en: You wrote this character correctly.

grammar_points.pattern: 那个人我没看清楚。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Nà ge rén wǒ méi kàn qīngchu.
grammar_points.explanation_en: I didn't see that person clearly.

segments.visual_notes: Show an equation-like animation: Verb + Complement = Result. For '卖完', show an image of a product being sold, followed by an 'out of stock' sign.

segments.teaching_goal: To explain the specific use of '好' as a complement to mean an action is completed and done well, or that one is ready for the next action.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on a special resultative complement: [zh:好]. When [zh:好] follows a verb, it means the action is not just finished, but also completed satisfactorily and you are ready for the next step. In the dialogue, the waiter says [zh:菜很快就能做好], 'The dishes will be ready soon.' This implies they will be cooked well and ready to be served. Another great example is [zh:饭做好了], which means 'The meal is ready.' It's more than just 'finished cooking'; it's 'ready to be eaten.' Similarly, [zh:功课做好了] means 'My homework is done,' implying you're now free to do something else.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Verb + 好 + 了
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Verb + hǎo + le
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Action completed and ready for the next step

grammar_points.pattern: 菜很快就能做好。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: cài hěn kuài jiù néng zuò hǎo
grammar_points.explanation_en: The dishes will be ready soon.

grammar_points.pattern: 饭做好了, 快来吃吧。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Fàn zuò hǎo le, kuài lái chī ba.
grammar_points.explanation_en: The food is ready. Come and eat.

grammar_points.pattern: 功课做好了, 我要睡觉了。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Gōngkè zuò hǎo le, wǒ yào shuì jiào le.
grammar_points.explanation_en: My homework is done, and I'm going to bed.

segments.visual_notes: Use a checkmark icon (✓) next to '好' to signify completion and satisfaction. Animate a sequence: '做饭' (cooking) → '饭做好了' (meal ready on table).

segments.teaching_goal: To practice asking and answering about task completion using the resultative complement '好'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's practice using [zh:好] to check on progress. To ask if someone has finished a task, you can use the pattern 'Task + [zh:好了吗]?' For instance, [zh:你汉字练习好了吗]? 'Have you finished practicing the characters?' To answer affirmatively, you can say [zh:汉字我练习好了]. For a negative response, you say [zh:汉字我没练习好]. Notice the negative uses [zh:没] and places it before the verb-complement phrase. A common mistake is to say something like [zh:我练习了不好], which is incorrect. The correct negative form is always [zh:没练习好].

on_screen_text.focus_text: Q: 你汉字练习好了吗？ A: 练习好了。/ 没练习好。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ Hànzì liànxí hǎo le ma? A: Liànxí hǎo le. / Méi liànxí hǎo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Q: Have you finished practicing the characters? A: I've finished. / I haven't finished.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The action of practicing (练习) is completed well (好).
The negative form, indicating the action is not yet completed.

segments.visual_notes: Display the question and then show two branching paths for the affirmative and negative answers, with a large green check for the correct negative form and a red X for the incorrect one.

segments.segment_title: Practice: Constructive Feedback with 多/少
segments.teaching_goal: To practice giving suggestions and instructions using the '多/少 + Verb' structure.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's do a quick drill on giving advice with [zh:多] and [zh:少]. This structure is perfect for making suggestions. Imagine a teacher giving feedback. They might say, [zh:老师希望学生多听录音], 'The teacher hopes the students listen to the recordings more.' Or, [zh:老师希望学生少玩儿], 'The teacher hopes the students play less.' The key is to always place [zh:多] or [zh:少] right before the verb phrase. A common error for English speakers is to place it after, like [zh:上课说多中文]. This is incorrect. Always remember, it's [zh:多说中文].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 老师希望学生 + [多/少] + Verb Phrase
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lǎoshī xīwàng xuésheng + [duō/shǎo] + Verb Phrase
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The teacher hopes the students do more/less of...

highlight_words.explanation_en: The instruction to increase the action of listening (听).
The instruction to decrease the action of speaking (说).

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a teacher giving advice. Animate '多' and '少' sliding into the correct position before the verb in the example sentence.

segments.teaching_goal: To review and consolidate the key grammar patterns and vocabulary from the lesson.

narration.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review what we've learned. We covered emphatic negation with 'not even one,' using [zh:一个都没]. We learned to say 'do more' or 'do less' by putting [zh:多] or [zh:少] before the verb. We also explored resultative complements like [zh:完] and [zh:好] to show the outcome of an action, and we distinguished between [zh:刚] and [zh:刚才]. Keep practicing these patterns to master ordering food in Chinese!

on_screen_text.focus_text: Key Phrases Review

production_notes.remarks: This schema is model-agnostic and designed for script planning plus downstream rendering.

video_style.teaching_style: A clear, step-by-step breakdown of dialogue, focusing on vocabulary and practical usage in a restaurant setting. The tone is encouraging and supportive.
video_style.visual_style: Clean, modern, with animated text and icons to support learning.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand and use a common welcome phrase in a restaurant.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The dialogue begins with the waiter welcoming the customers. They say [zh:请进,请进]. [zh:请] means 'please,' and [zh:进] means 'to enter.' So, [zh:请进] literally means 'please enter.' Repeating it makes the welcome sound more enthusiastic and friendly. This is a very common and polite way to greet customers entering a shop or restaurant.

content.focus_text: 请进,请进。
content.focus_pinyin: qǐng jìn, qǐng jìn.
content.focus_gloss_en: Come in, please come in.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A polite word used to make requests or invite someone to do something.
A verb indicating movement into a space.
A polite word used to make requests or invite someone to do something.
A verb indicating movement into a space.

segments.visual_notes: Animation of a restaurant door opening with the characters walking in.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express surprise about a situation and observe a lack of something.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You is surprised by how busy the restaurant is. She says, [zh:人怎么这么多]. This means 'How come there are so many people?'. [zh:怎么] here expresses surprise. Then she says, [zh:好像一个位子都没有了]. [zh:好像] means 'it seems,' and [zh:位子] means 'seat.' The full phrase means 'It seems there isn't a single seat left.' This is an emphatic way to say there are no seats.

content.focus_text: 人怎么这么多？好像一个位子都没有了。
content.focus_pinyin: rén zěnme zhème duō? hǎoxiàng yí ge wèizi dōu méiyǒu le.
content.focus_gloss_en: How come there are so many people? It seems there are no seats left.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used here to express surprise or ask for a reason.
Used to express an impression or observation.
Refers to a place to sit, especially in a public venue like a restaurant or theater.
Used here to express surprise or ask for a reason.
Used to express an impression or observation.
Refers to a place to sit, especially in a public venue like a restaurant or theater.

segments.visual_notes: Show a bustling restaurant scene. Highlight the phrase '一个...都没' to draw attention to the structure.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to get a waiter's attention and ask if seats are available.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng addresses the waiter to ask for a table. He starts with [zh:服务员], which means 'waiter' or 'attendant.' Then he politely asks, [zh:请问,还有没有位子]. [zh:请问] is 'excuse me, may I ask...'. The question [zh:还有没有位子] uses the 'verb-not-verb' structure to ask 'are there still seats or not?'. It's a very common way to ask a yes-no question.

content.focus_text: 服务员,请问,还有没有位子？
content.focus_pinyin: fúwùyuán, qǐngwèn, hái yǒu méiyǒu wèizi?
content.focus_gloss_en: Waiter, excuse me, are there any seats available?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A general term for service staff in places like restaurants, hotels, or airports.
Refers to a place to sit.
A general term for service staff in places like restaurants, hotels, or airports.
Refers to a place to sit.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a character raising their hand to call the waiter. Highlight the '有没有' structure.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a positive response and how to identify an object using measure words.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The waiter responds positively. The triple [zh:有,有,有] is an emphatic 'Yes, yes, yes!'. Then, the waiter points out a free table: [zh:那张桌子没有人]. [zh:那] means 'that.' [zh:张] is a measure word used for flat objects like tables, paper, or beds. [zh:桌子] is 'table.' The phrase [zh:没有人] literally means 'has no people,' which in this context means the table is free or unoccupied.

content.focus_text: 有,有,有。那张桌子没有人。
content.focus_pinyin: yǒu, yǒu, yǒu. nà zhāng zhuōzi méiyǒu rén.
content.focus_gloss_en: Yes, yes, yes. That table is free.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used for tables, paper, beds, etc.
A piece of furniture with a flat top and one or more legs.
Used for tables, paper, beds, etc.
A piece of furniture with a flat top and one or more legs.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the waiter pointing to an empty table. A label with '那张桌子' appears above the table.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the standard phrase a waiter uses to ask what customers want to eat.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now that they are seated, the waiter asks what they'd like to eat. The phrase is [zh:两位想吃点儿什么]. Let's break it down. [zh:两] means 'two.' [zh:位] is a polite measure word for people. [zh:想] means 'would like to.' [zh:吃点儿什么] means 'eat a little something?'. This is a very common and polite way for service staff to ask for your order.

content.focus_text: 两位想吃点儿什么？
content.focus_pinyin: liǎng wèi xiǎng chī diǎnr shénme?
content.focus_gloss_en: What would you two like to eat?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used instead of '二' (èr) when counting people or things with a measure word.
A more formal and polite alternative to '个' (ge) when referring to people.
Used instead of '二' (èr) when counting people or things with a measure word.
A more formal and polite alternative to '个' (ge) when referring to people.

segments.visual_notes: Show the waiter holding a notepad, ready to take the order. Highlight '两位' to emphasize the polite form.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to suggest someone else do something.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You suggests that Wang Peng should order the food. She says, [zh:你点菜吧]. The key phrase here is [zh:点菜], which is a verb-object compound meaning 'to order dishes.' The particle [zh:吧] at the end of the sentence softens the tone and turns it into a suggestion, like 'Why don't you order?' or 'Go ahead and order.'

content.focus_text: 王朋,你点菜吧。
content.focus_pinyin: Wáng Péng, nǐ diǎn cài ba.
content.focus_gloss_en: Wang Peng, why don't you order?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'to point at dishes,' this is the standard verb for ordering food from a menu.
Literally 'to point at dishes,' this is the standard verb for ordering food from a menu.

segments.visual_notes: Animate Li You gesturing towards Wang Peng with the menu.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to place an order for a specific dish and specify a preference.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng starts ordering. He says, [zh:先给我们两盘饺子]. [zh:先] means 'first.' [zh:两盘饺子] means 'two plates of dumplings.' [zh:盘] is the measure word for 'plate.' Then he adds a specification: [zh:要素的]. [zh:要] means 'to want,' and [zh:素的] means 'vegetarian ones.' This is a concise way to specify that he wants the vegetarian version of the dish.

content.focus_text: 好。先给我们两盘饺子,要素的。
content.focus_pinyin: hǎo. xiān gěi wǒmen liǎng pán jiǎozi, yào sù de.
content.focus_gloss_en: Okay. First, give us two plates of dumplings, vegetarian ones.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used for food served on a plate.
A popular Chinese dish consisting of minced meat and/or vegetables wrapped in a thin piece of dough.
Used to describe food that does not contain meat.
Used for food served on a plate.
A popular Chinese dish consisting of minced meat and/or vegetables wrapped in a thin piece of dough.
Used to describe food that does not contain meat.

segments.visual_notes: Show an image of two plates of dumplings. A 'vegetarian' icon can pop up next to them.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a common structure for asking 'besides this, what else?'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The waiter asks what else they would like. The sentence uses the structure [zh:除了...以外]. This means 'besides' or 'in addition to.' So, [zh:除了饺子以外] means 'besides the dumplings.' The question [zh:还要什么] means 'what else do you want?'. This is a very useful pattern for adding more items to an order or list.

content.focus_text: 除了饺子以外,还要什么？
content.focus_pinyin: chúle jiǎozi yǐwài, hái yào shénme?
content.focus_gloss_en: Besides dumplings, what else would you like?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A structure used to talk about something in addition to what has already been mentioned.
Indicates continuation or addition.
A structure used to talk about something in addition to what has already been mentioned.
Indicates continuation or addition.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the '除了...以外' structure, showing how it frames '饺子'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a simple phrase to ask for someone's opinion.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng turns to Li You to ask for her input. He says, [zh:李友, 你说呢]. This is a very simple and common way to ask for someone's opinion. [zh:你说] means 'you say,' and the particle [zh:呢] turns it into a question, similar to 'What do you say?' or 'And you?'. It's a way of passing the turn to someone else.

content.focus_text: 李友,你说呢？
content.focus_pinyin: Lǐ Yǒu, nǐ shuō ne?
content.focus_gloss_en: Li You, what do you say?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common verb for speaking.
A common verb for speaking.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a question mark appearing over Li You's head.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to order another dish, make a negative request, and state a dietary preference.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You adds her order. She wants [zh:一盘家常豆腐], one plate of home-style tofu. Then she makes a special request: [zh:不要放肉], which means 'don't put meat in it.' [zh:放] is the verb 'to put.' Finally, she explains why: [zh:我吃素]. This phrase means 'I eat vegetarian' or 'I am a vegetarian.' It's a very important phrase for anyone with dietary restrictions.

content.focus_text: 还要一盘家常豆腐,不要放肉,我吃素。
content.focus_pinyin: hái yào yì pán jiācháng dòufu, bú yào fàng ròu, wǒ chīsù.
content.focus_gloss_en: I also want a plate of home-style tofu, without meat. I'm a vegetarian.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common tofu dish. '家常' means 'home-style'.
A verb used here to mean 'to add an ingredient'.
A general term for meat.
A common tofu dish. '家常' means 'home-style'.
A verb used here to mean 'to add an ingredient'.
A general term for meat.
Literally 'to eat vegetarian food'.

segments.visual_notes: Show an image of home-style tofu. Animate a 'no meat' icon appearing next to it.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a simple sentence confirming the contents of a dish.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The waiter confirms that the dish meets Li You's requirement. They say, [zh:我们的家常豆腐没有肉]. This is a straightforward sentence. [zh:我们] means 'our.' [zh:的] is a possessive particle. So, [zh:我们的家常豆腐] means 'our home-style tofu.' The phrase [zh:没有肉] simply means 'doesn't have meat.' This reassures Li You that the dish is vegetarian.

content.focus_text: 我们的家常豆腐没有肉。
content.focus_pinyin: wǒmen de jiācháng dòufu méiyǒu ròu.
content.focus_gloss_en: Our home-style tofu doesn't have meat.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The negative form of '有' (yǒu), meaning 'to have'.
The negative form of '有' (yǒu), meaning 'to have'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to order soup and make multiple specific requests about ingredients.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You continues her order with more details. First, [zh:还要两碗酸辣汤] means 'also want two bowls of hot and sour soup.' [zh:碗] is the measure word for 'bowl.' Then come two requests: [zh:请别放味精], 'please don't add MSG,' and [zh:少放点儿盐], 'add a little less salt.' Notice the use of [zh:少] to mean 'less.' Finally, she asks, [zh:有小白菜吗], 'Do you have baby bok choy?'

content.focus_text: 还要两碗酸辣汤,请别放味精,少放点儿盐。有小白菜吗？
content.focus_pinyin: hái yào liǎng wǎn suānlàtāng, qǐng bié fàng wèijīng, shǎo fàng diǎnr yán. yǒu xiǎo báicài ma?
content.focus_gloss_en: Also want two bowls of hot and sour soup. Please don't add MSG, and use less salt. Do you have baby bok choy?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used for food served in a bowl, like soup or rice.
A popular Chinese soup. '酸' means sour, '辣' means spicy, and '汤' means soup.
A common flavor enhancer.
Used for food served in a bowl, like soup or rice.
A popular Chinese soup. '酸' means sour, '辣' means spicy, and '汤' means soup.
A common flavor enhancer.
Used before a verb to mean 'do something less'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to say something is sold out using a resultative complement.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The waiter apologizes because they don't have the vegetable. They say, [zh:对不起], which means 'sorry.' Then, [zh:小白菜刚卖完]. [zh:刚] means 'just now.' The key part is [zh:卖完]. This is a verb with a resultative complement. [zh:卖] means 'to sell,' and [zh:完] means 'finished' or 'done.' Together, [zh:卖完] means 'to be sold out.' This is a very common and useful structure.

content.focus_text: 对不起,小白菜刚卖完。
content.focus_pinyin: duìbuqǐ, xiǎo báicài gāng mài wán.
content.focus_gloss_en: Sorry, the baby bok choy is just sold out.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb indicating that an action took place in the very recent past.
The verb for selling goods.
Used as a complement after a verb to indicate the completion of an action.
An adverb indicating that an action took place in the very recent past.
The verb for selling goods.
Used as a complement after a verb to indicate the completion of an action.

segments.visual_notes: Show a picture of bok choy with a 'Sold Out' sign stamped over it.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to cancel part of an order.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Since the bok choy is unavailable, Li You decides to skip the vegetables. She says, [zh:那就不要青菜了]. [zh:那就] means 'in that case' or 'then.' [zh:不要] means 'don't want.' [zh:青菜] is a general term for green leafy vegetables. The particle [zh:了] at the end indicates a change of situation or decision. So, the sentence means 'Then we'll do without green vegetables.'

content.focus_text: 那就不要青菜了。
content.focus_pinyin: nà jiù bú yào qīngcài le.
content.focus_gloss_en: Then we'll do without green vegetables.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A general term for vegetables like bok choy, spinach, etc.
A general term for vegetables like bok choy, spinach, etc.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the bok choy being crossed off the order list.

segments.teaching_goal: To recognize the standard question for asking about drinks.

narration_track.subtitle_en: With the food order settled, the waiter moves on to drinks. They ask, [zh:那喝点儿什么呢]. [zh:那] means 'then' or 'in that case.' The core question is [zh:喝点儿什么], 'drink a little something?'. [zh:喝] is the verb 'to drink.' The particle [zh:呢] at the end makes the question softer and more conversational. This is the standard way to ask what someone wants to drink.

content.focus_text: 那喝点儿什么呢？
content.focus_pinyin: nà hē diǎnr shénme ne?
content.focus_gloss_en: What would you like to drink then?

highlight_words.explanation_en: The verb for consuming liquids.
The verb for consuming liquids.

segments.visual_notes: Show icons of various drinks like tea, soda, and water.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to order a drink and ask someone else what they want.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng orders his drink first. He says, [zh:我要一杯冰茶]. [zh:我要] means 'I want.' [zh:一杯] means 'one glass of.' [zh:杯] is the measure word for 'cup' or 'glass.' [zh:冰茶] is 'iced tea.' After ordering for himself, he asks Li You, [zh:你喝什么], which simply means 'What are you drinking?'

content.focus_text: 我要一杯冰茶。李友,你喝什么？
content.focus_pinyin: wǒ yào yī bēi bīngchá. Lǐ Yǒu, nǐ hē shénme?
content.focus_gloss_en: I'd like a glass of iced tea. Li You, what are you drinking?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used for drinks served in a cup or glass.
'冰' means ice, and '茶' means tea.
Used for drinks served in a cup or glass.
'冰' means ice, and '茶' means tea.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a glass of iced tea appearing next to Wang Peng.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express thirst and make a specific request for 'more' of something.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You explains she is thirsty: [zh:我很渴]. [zh:渴] is the adjective for 'thirsty.' Then she orders: [zh:请给我一杯可乐], 'Please give me a glass of Coke.' She adds a special request: [zh:多放点儿冰]. [zh:多] means 'more.' Used before the verb [zh:放] (to put), it means 'put more' or 'add extra.' [zh:冰] means 'ice.' So, she wants extra ice in her drink.

content.focus_text: 我很渴,请给我一杯可乐,多放点儿冰。
content.focus_pinyin: wǒ hěn kě, qǐng gěi wǒ yī bēi kělè, duō fàng diǎnr bīng.
content.focus_gloss_en: I'm very thirsty. Please give me a Coke, with extra ice.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The feeling of needing to drink.
A phonetic borrowing for 'cola'.
Used before a verb to mean 'do something more'.
The feeling of needing to drink.
A phonetic borrowing for 'cola'.
Used before a verb to mean 'do something more'.
Frozen water.

segments.visual_notes: Show a glass of coke being filled with extra ice cubes.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how a waiter confirms a full order and asks a final confirmation question.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The waiter repeats the entire order to confirm it's correct. This is a great review of all the food items and measure words: [zh:两盘饺子], [zh:一盘家常豆腐], [zh:两碗酸辣汤], [zh:一杯冰茶], and [zh:一杯可乐]. They also confirm the special request, [zh:多放冰]. Finally, they ask one last time, [zh:还要别的吗], which means 'Anything else?'. [zh:别的] means 'other things'.

content.focus_text: 好,两盘饺子,一盘家常豆腐,两碗酸辣汤,一杯冰茶,一杯可乐,多放冰。还要别的吗？
content.focus_pinyin: hǎo, liǎng pán jiǎozi, yī pán jiācháng dòufu, liǎng wǎn suānlàtāng, yī bēi bīngchá, yī bēi kělè, duō fàng bīng. hái yào biéde ma?
content.focus_gloss_en: Okay, two plates of dumplings, one plate of home-style tofu, two bowls of hot and sour soup, one iced tea, one Coke with extra ice. Anything else?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to other, different things.
Refers to other, different things.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a checklist appearing on screen, with each item being ticked off as the waiter says it.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to confirm an order is complete and ask for faster service.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You confirms the order is complete and makes a request. She says, [zh:不要别的了], 'Nothing else,' and [zh:这些够了], 'This is enough.' [zh:够] means 'enough.' Then she adds that they are hungry: [zh:我们都饿了]. [zh:饿] means 'hungry.' Because they are hungry, she asks, [zh:请上菜快一点儿]. [zh:上菜] means 'to serve the food,' and [zh:快一点儿] means 'a little faster.' This is a polite way to ask for quick service.

content.focus_text: 不要别的了,这些够了。服务员,我们都饿了,请上菜快一点儿。
content.focus_pinyin: bú yào bié de le, zhè xiē gòu le. fú wù yuán, wǒ men dōu è le, qǐng shàng cài kuài yì diǎnr.
content.focus_gloss_en: Nothing else, this is enough. Waiter, we are both hungry, please serve the food a little faster.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates that the quantity is sufficient.
The feeling of needing to eat.
Literally 'to bring up the dishes'.
Indicates that the quantity is sufficient.
The feeling of needing to eat.
Literally 'to bring up the dishes'.
An adjective for speed.

segments.visual_notes: Show a character's stomach rumbling. Then show a clock with its hands speeding up.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a common phrase for 'no problem' and how to say something will be ready soon.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The waiter reassures them. They start with [zh:没问题], a very common and useful phrase that means 'no problem.' Then they say, [zh:菜很快就能做好]. [zh:菜] is 'dishes.' [zh:很快] means 'very soon.' The final part, [zh:做好], means 'to be finished' or 'to be ready.' The verb [zh:做] means 'to make,' and the complement [zh:好] indicates the action is completed well and ready for the next step, which is eating!

content.focus_text: 没问题,菜很快就能做好。
content.focus_pinyin: méi wèn tí, cài hěn kuài jiù néng zuò hǎo.
content.focus_gloss_en: No problem, the dishes will be ready soon.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common expression used to agree to a request or to reassure someone.
'好' is used as a resultative complement to indicate that the action '做' (to do/make) is completed successfully.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students how to express 'not at all' or 'not even one' for emphatic negation.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's look at our first grammar point: emphatic negation. In the dialogue, Li You says [zh:好像一个位子都没有了], meaning 'It seems there isn't a single seat left.' This structure, '一 + measure word + noun + 都/也 + 没/不', is used to strongly negate something. It means 'not even one'. For countable nouns, you must use a measure word, like [zh:个] for [zh:位子]. For uncountable nouns, you can use [zh:一点儿]. For example, [zh:妈妈做菜一点儿味精都不放] means 'Mom doesn't use any MSG at all in her cooking.' This is a very common and useful pattern to emphasize a total lack of something.

content.focus_text: 一个 + [Measure Word] + [Noun] + 都/也 + 没有
content.focus_pinyin: yí ge + [Measure Word] + [Noun] + dōu/yě + méiyǒu
content.focus_gloss_en: Not even one [Noun]

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students how to use '多' and '少' before verbs to mean 'do more' or 'do less' of an action.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Next, let's learn how to say 'do more' or 'do less' of something. In Chinese, you place the words [zh:多] for 'more' or [zh:少] for 'less' directly before the verb. In the dialogue, Li You asks the waiter to [zh:少放点儿盐], 'add less salt,' and [zh:多放点儿冰], 'add more ice.' This is different from English where 'more' or 'less' often comes after the verb. Remember the structure: [zh:多] or [zh:少] plus the verb. For example, in Chinese class, your teacher might say [zh:上中文课得多说中文，少说英文], meaning 'In Chinese class, you should speak more Chinese and less English.'

content.focus_text: [多/少] + Verb
content.focus_pinyin: [duō/shǎo] + Verb
content.focus_gloss_en: Do more/less of an action

segments.teaching_goal: To help students differentiate between the adverb '刚' (just) and the time word '刚才' (just now).

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now let's clarify two similar-sounding words: [zh:刚] and [zh:刚才]. Both relate to the recent past, but they are used differently. [zh:刚] is an adverb meaning 'just,' and it describes an action that happened a very short time ago. In the dialogue, the waiter says [zh:小白菜刚卖完], 'The baby bok choy just sold out.' In contrast, [zh:刚才] is a time word meaning 'just now' or 'a moment ago.' A key difference is that you cannot use negative words like [zh:不] or [zh:没] after [zh:刚]. Also, sentences with [zh:刚] cannot end with [zh:了]. For example, you can say [zh:他刚才在这儿], 'He was here a moment ago,' but not [zh:他刚在这儿了].

content.focus_text: Q: 小白菜还有吗？ A: 小白菜刚卖完。
content.focus_pinyin: Q: Xiǎo báicài hái yǒu ma? A: Xiǎo báicài gāng mài wán.
content.focus_gloss_en: Q: Is there any baby bok choy left? A: The baby bok choy just sold out.

segments.teaching_goal: To introduce the concept of resultative complements, which indicate the result of a verb's action.

narration_track.subtitle_en: A very important concept in Chinese grammar is the resultative complement. This is when an adjective or another verb follows a verb to show the result of the action. Let's look at the line [zh:小白菜刚卖完]. Here, the verb is [zh:卖], 'to sell,' and the complement is [zh:完], 'to finish.' Together, [zh:卖完] means 'to sell out'—the action of selling is finished. Other common complements include [zh:错] for 'wrong,' as in [zh:找错钱了], 'gave the wrong change,' and [zh:对] for 'correct,' as in [zh:写对了], 'wrote correctly.' The negative form is simple: just put [zh:没] or [zh:没有] before the verb, like [zh:小白菜没卖完], 'The baby bok choy is not sold out.'

content.focus_text: Verb + Resultative Complement
content.focus_gloss_en: Indicates the result of an action

segments.teaching_goal: To explain the specific use of '好' as a complement to mean an action is completed and done well, or that one is ready for the next action.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's focus on a special resultative complement: [zh:好]. When [zh:好] follows a verb, it means the action is not just finished, but also completed satisfactorily and you are ready for the next step. In the dialogue, the waiter says [zh:菜很快就能做好], 'The dishes will be ready soon.' This implies they will be cooked well and ready to be served. Another great example is [zh:饭做好了], which means 'The meal is ready.' It's more than just 'finished cooking'; it's 'ready to be eaten.' Similarly, [zh:功课做好了] means 'My homework is done,' implying you're now free to do something else.

segments.teaching_goal: To practice asking and answering about task completion using the resultative complement '好'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's practice using [zh:好] to check on progress. To ask if someone has finished a task, you can use the pattern 'Task + [zh:好了吗]?' For instance, [zh:你汉字练习好了吗]? 'Have you finished practicing the characters?' To answer affirmatively, you can say [zh:汉字我练习好了]. For a negative response, you say [zh:汉字我没练习好]. Notice the negative uses [zh:没] and places it before the verb-complement phrase. A common mistake is to say something like [zh:我练习了不好], which is incorrect. The correct negative form is always [zh:没练习好].

content.focus_text: Q: 你汉字练习好了吗？ A: 练习好了。/ 没练习好。
content.focus_pinyin: Nǐ Hànzì liànxí hǎo le ma? A: Liànxí hǎo le. / Méi liànxí hǎo.
content.focus_gloss_en: Q: Have you finished practicing the characters? A: I've finished. / I haven't finished.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The action of practicing (练习) is completed well (好).
The negative form, indicating the action is not yet completed.

segments.segment_title: Practice: Constructive Feedback with 多/少
segments.teaching_goal: To practice giving suggestions and instructions using the '多/少 + Verb' structure.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's do a quick drill on giving advice with [zh:多] and [zh:少]. This structure is perfect for making suggestions. Imagine a teacher giving feedback. They might say, [zh:老师希望学生多听录音], 'The teacher hopes the students listen to the recordings more.' Or, [zh:老师希望学生少玩儿], 'The teacher hopes the students play less.' The key is to always place [zh:多] or [zh:少] right before the verb phrase. A common error for English speakers is to place it after, like [zh:上课说多中文]. This is incorrect. Always remember, it's [zh:多说中文].

content.focus_text: 老师希望学生 + [多/少] + Verb Phrase
content.focus_pinyin: Lǎoshī xīwàng xuésheng + [duō/shǎo] + Verb Phrase
content.focus_gloss_en: The teacher hopes the students do more/less of...

highlight_words.explanation_en: The instruction to increase the action of listening (听).
The instruction to decrease the action of speaking (说).

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a teacher giving advice. Animate '多' and '少' sliding into the correct position before the verb in the example sentence.

segments.teaching_goal: To review and consolidate the key grammar patterns and vocabulary from the lesson.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review what we've learned. We covered emphatic negation with 'not even one,' using [zh:一个都没]. We learned to say 'do more' or 'do less' by putting [zh:多] or [zh:少] before the verb. We also explored resultative complements like [zh:完] and [zh:好] to show the outcome of an action, and we distinguished between [zh:刚] and [zh:刚才]. Keep practicing these patterns to master ordering food in Chinese!
